recent.notes: Cal Athletics laid off ~25 staff in marketing, athletic communications and creative services units, effective early June 2026. Framed as "complete redefinition" of external operations transitioning to new "Strawberry Creek Studios" revenue generation model. Driven by $24.3M projected shortfall for 2024-25 fiscal year and broader college sports landscape changes (conference realignment, transfer portal, NIL). Co-ADs Jenny Simon-O'Neill and Jay Larson announced cuts April 11. Athletics department action; no academic programs affected. New football coach Tosh Lupoi hired December 2025.

Additionally cutting 41 already-vacant positions. Driven by flat state appropriations (3 consecutive years), 1.8% projected enrollment decline, and 11% rising healthcare costs. York campus buildings being sold. Budget deficit projected near $10M without intervention.

93 programs sunsetted (closed or paused) out of ~460 total following 7-month academic portfolio review begun August 2025. Breakdown: 41 bachelor's degrees, 19 graduate degrees, 33 certificates (28 advanced). 55 of the 93 had zero enrolled students. 258 students affected (1.2% of 22K+ enrollment); all may complete degrees via teach-out. No positions eliminated. Affected programs include classics, ceramics, sculpture, painting, German, Italian, French, Russian, Middle Eastern studies, African American studies, digital humanities, Modern Jewish studies, and others. Driven by enrollment concentration: 34% of programs serve 80% of students. Explicitly not a cost-cutting exercise per provost. Chancellor Kent Syverud departing May 10 for University of Michigan.

Multiple program discontinuations and restructurings as UNO transitions to LSU New Orleans in 2026. Discontinued: BA International Studies (→ minor), BS Elementary Ed & Special Ed (Grades 1-5), MAT Elementary Ed & Special Ed (Grades 1-5), MAT Secondary Ed & Special Ed (Grades 6-12), MFA theatre-related tracks/concentrations. Restructured: BA Art History → BA Studio Art (Art History as minor); BA Film and Theatre → BA Film Arts (Theatre Arts as minor); MFA renamed. Taught out: BS Mathematics (while new BS Data Science is developed); MS Tax Accounting (existing teach-out continues). MA Arts Administration merged into MPA. Less than 2% of students affected. All faculty retained during transition. New programs under development: engineering management, hospitality and tourism, data science, healthcare management.

Non-renewal letters sent by Provost Dr. Ryan Sandefer citing "current financial constraints" and institutional restructuring. Sources indicate more than a dozen faculty affected; college declined to confirm any personnel figures. College described cuts as "necessary budget adjustments." Letters stated non-renewals do not reflect negatively on performance. Catholic Benedictine private institution in Duluth, MN. Student sit-in protest reported following cuts including a popular philosophy professor.

Board unanimously voted to eliminate 10 career and technical education (CTE) programs, including all architecture programs and one certificate each from several other academic areas. Reasons cited: low enrollment, non-transferable credits, low completion rates, and lack of industry demand. No full-time faculty negatively impacted; no adjuncts losing jobs as they typically teach across multiple programs. Budget pressures driven by statewide tuition freeze and declining property tax revenue from appraisal freeze. Chancellor LeBlanc described cuts as addressing "obsolete" programs amid a "new reality" for the college budget.

All 9 staff at Keller Center for Innovation in Engineering Education laid off. Tenured faculty salaries frozen; all other staff raises capped at 1%. All departments cut 5-10% of budgets. Most staff hiring frozen. Driven by revised endowment return projection (8% vs historical 10.2-12%), translating to ~$11.3B less in endowment value over 10 years and ~$500M less in annual payouts. Additional pressures: rising healthcare costs, federal research grant suspensions, new 8% endowment tax. Endowment funds 65% of operating budget ($36.4B total). President Eisgruber warned more cuts are coming. Graduate stipends and undergraduate financial aid commitments preserved.

70+ programs, minors and certificates cut or consolidated to address $45M deficit. Major cuts: all linguistics degrees phased out (dept merged with World Languages, Literatures and Cultures effective Sept. 1); BA in Latino and Latin American Studies eliminated; MA in Women's and Gender Studies cut. 25 undergraduate minors eliminated including women's and gender studies, LGBTQ studies, Mexican American studies, Africana studies, Asian studies, dance, geology, and special education. 40+ certificate programs cut (avg. fewer than 2 students/year). Driven by sharp decline in international enrollment and reduced state funding. Faculty buyouts offered to those with 15+ years service (deadline April 10). Linguistics dept chair said faculty were not consulted. Faculty and staff impact TBD. Enrolled students may complete degrees; no new admissions to affected programs. ~47,000 total enrollment.

17 programs sunset per Ohio Advance Higher Education Act (SB1) mandate requiring 5+ annual degree conferrals averaged over 3 years. Originally 36 programs fell below threshold; 16 approved to merge with similar programs, 3 received ODHE waivers, 17 sunset. Programs closed include BA in African American Studies, BA in French, BA in Chemistry, BA in Dance, BFA in Interdisciplinary Arts, BM in Music Therapy, BA in Mathematics, BA in Physics, BA in Geological Sciences, and several associate degrees. Most disciplines continue via minors, BS alternatives or related programs. 16 additional programs being merged/combined. Currently enrolled students may complete degrees as outlined. Incoming applicants to be notified of alternatives.

Board of Trustees voted to permanently close the college at the end of Fall 2026. Announced by President Jennifer Chrisler on March 14, 2026. Driven by $20M+ in debt, $3.7M operating deficit (2025), chronic enrollment decline since 2019 (when college did not admit an incoming class), and NECHE accreditor sustainability requirement. Five-year sustainability plan (2019-2024) fell short: raised $55M of $60M goal but could not grow enrollment sufficiently. Two student pathways offered: Completion (Division III students finish at Hampshire through Dec 2026) and Transfer (to Five College Consortium partners and others). Founded 1965; known for independent liberal arts model. Board stated closure decision came only after exhausting all alternatives; nearly all trustees are alumni.

15% of full-time faculty and staff to be laid off by mid-June 2026. Driven by $48M projected deficit and ~20% enrollment decline since 2021. Prior voluntary buyouts offered to 40% of full-time faculty (Dec 2025) made only a 7% dent. AAUP described this as the largest attempted faculty firing currently in the nation. Restructuring includes Parsons merging with College of Performing Arts, and Eugene Lang College merging with New School for Social Research (NSSR). Layoffs disproportionately affect NSSR and Lang due to greater program consolidation. Full-time faculty union organizing effort underway. Target: balanced budget by 2027-28.

School of Arts and Sciences sent non-reappointment letters to 38 adjunct lecturers on March 6 — the contractual notification deadline — affecting ~100 classes. University characterized as "precautionary" with possibility of rehiring. REVERSED: All 37 lecturers rehired following pushback from Rutgers Adjunct Faculty Union and department chairs. Union disputed financial necessity, citing endowment >$2B, 50%+ growth in unrestricted reserves since pandemic, and 3%+ enrollment growth. Cuts would have saved ~$1.2M against a $5.9B annual budget.

NJCU announced the reduction of its academic portfolio by 37 percent, which involved sunsetting 48 undergraduate programs, 24 minors, 28 graduate programs, 10 certificate programs, and one doctoral program.

At least 16 faculty laid off, mostly in humanities, effective end of academic year (pay and benefits through August). Board of trustees approved cuts in early February 2026. Driven by Ohio Advance Higher Education Act (SB1) compliance — requires eliminating programs with fewer than 5 graduates/year over 3 years — and ongoing fiscal distress including a $26M student tuition/fee backlog. Ohio Dept. of Higher Education placed CSU on fiscal watch in 2024. Prior year cuts included 33 staff and 7 faculty. HBCU; secured $400K+ in emergency student aid via UNCF partnership in January 2026.

Board unanimously voted to eliminate 9 minors and 7 concentrations (16 total), saving ~$400K. Minors cut include African American Studies, Cybersecurity, Economics, Latin American Studies, Management Information Systems, Non-profit Studies, Peace and Justice Studies, Philosophy and Religion, and Professional Writing. Current students with declared minors/concentrations may finish via teach-out. Second round of cuts after 6 programs were eliminated in May 2025. Part of effort to close ~$8M shortfall. Combined with fee increases, generates ~$800K. 4 athletic teams (men's/women's swimming and tennis) also recommended for elimination but decision deferred to May 2026 board meeting. Tuition increase not pursued for 2026-27.

Board voted 4-2 to lay off 16 non-faculty workers and eliminate 17 vacant positions (33 total). Adaptive Physical Education (APE) program discontinued beginning 2026-27; offered for 40+ years serving ~200 disabled students. Cuts also include 3 Disability Support Programs roles. Driven by loss of HSI federal grants, stagnating state funding, and local property tax underperformance. Projected deficit of $3.75M in 2027 and $4.4M in 2028 without action. Board declared financial hardship via resolution.

Deactivating 1 undergraduate program (Environmental Geography, B.S.), 2 graduate programs (Conflict Analysis and Resolution, M.S.; Higher Education and Student Affairs Administration, M.S.), 2 minors, and 3 certificates. 19 students in impacted bachelor's programs, 29 in master's programs (48 total; 0.8% of enrollment). Enrolled students may complete degrees via teach-out. Part of SUNY-mandated Framework for Fiscal Stability Plan targeting elimination of $16-17M structural deficit by 2027-28. Deficit attributed to Great Recession-era state cuts and COVID-19 financial losses. Prior year cuts included sociology. Faculty union UUP held campus rally March 10 demanding state funding.

22 staff laid off (18 full-time, 4 part-time) via reorganizations, staffing consolidations and reductions across administrative offices. Majority are non-student-facing roles. No faculty affected. Enrollment has dropped from 2,033 in 2011-12 to 1,600+ currently. Driven by declining birth rates, fewer students choosing college, and reduced international enrollment. Endowment remains strong at ~$370M. Board of Trustees approved to return college to balanced budget.

45 total positions eliminated (12 faculty, 21 staff, 11 administrators; ~40% currently vacant). College of Education (est. 1958) dissolved and absorbed into new College of Humanities, Education and Social Science (CHESS), saving ~$1.8M. Biology dept split: biochemistry, microbiology, and anatomy & physiology merged with pharmacy's pharmaceutical sciences into new Dept of Biomedical Sciences under Kasiska Division of Health Sciences. Driven by $8M deficit from 3% state-mandated budget cut plus $3M health insurance increases and $2.2M in unfunded cost adjustments. Accreditation concerns raised by former College of Education dean.

All ~40 college-employed dining staff positions eliminated after spring 2026 semester; staff may apply to continue with dining contractor Bon Appétit. Early retirement incentive offered to all employees 60+ with 10+ years of service (2 weeks pay per year worked, up to 18 months). Driven by missed enrollment goals for 2 consecutive years. No faculty positions affected. Tuition benefit preserved for early retirees with currently enrolled dependents.
Eliminated approximately 70 classified and management positions due to financial crisis.

Women's and Gender Studies (WGST) program closed effective immediately per Interim President Tommy Williams. Impacts BA/BS degrees, undergraduate minor, and graduate certificate. 25 enrolled majors and 31 enrolled minors (56 total) will be taught out over up to 6 semesters. 50+ affiliated faculty employment status unaffected. Cited Texas A&M System policy compliance and limited enrollment. Part of broader spring 2026 course review covering ~5,400 syllabi; 6 courses also canceled for non-compliance.

Proposed termination of MA in Literature (CIP 23.0101, ~8 students) and MA in Economics (CIP 45.0601, ~5 students). Moratorium on minors in Chinese Studies (~10 students) and Irish Studies (~7 students). Driven by declining enrollment and resource reallocation to higher strategic priority programs. Teach-out planned for remaining students. Pending Faculty Senate and Commissioner of Higher Education approval.

PennWest proposed a broad academic restructuring that would reduce bachelor's degree majors from 47 to 43, minors from 74 to 40, and master's degree options from 27 to 23, while increasing undergraduate certificates from 19 to 37. More than 100 academic offerings were previously recommended for moratorium, meaning programs would stop accepting new students while current students could finish. The proposal was still under review by university leadership at the time of reporting.

Eastern Washington University announced the discontinuation of its Division I adaptive athletics program (wheelchair basketball) at the end of the 2025-26 academic season due to sustainability and financial challenges. The program was the first of its kind in the Pacific Northwest.
